content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Romance movies for straight guys?

**Score:** 37
**Body:** Please suggest some good romance movies that I can watch. I am a straight male if that means anything. I am just starting to enjoy stuff that isn’t ’manly’ and that includes me liking the romance movies I have seen. Which are: Princess Bride, 500 Days of Summer, People We Meet On Vacation, and a couple of Disney movies. People We Meet On Vacation is easily one of my favorites. Outside of movies, I really like Fragrant Flower Blooms with Dignity  and Quintessential Quintuplets for the anime fans out there. Movie recommendations and anime recommendations would be nice! Thank you! Something that encapsulates real life really well and isn’t too cringy.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Grosse Pointe Blank
2. I think 10 Things I Hate About You is a very good “unisex” romance movie. 

Forgetting Sarah Marshall is a bit more of a breakup movie than a romance movie but I think it qualifies as both, and pretty much anyone can enjoy it as long as you’re okay with seeing Jason Segel’s penis. 
3. Stranger Than Fiction, Dan in Real Life, Crazy Stupid Love
4. Romancing The Stone 
5. Crazy, Stupid Love is a pretty good film. 
6. Alright. I want to thank everyone for their recs. I truly cannot respond to everyone this fast. I did not expect to many people to see my post. Bout clearly, yall did. Thanks. If/when I watch it, I will get back to you. Just know that it’s not easy for me to watch new things (ik how ironic is it that I’m asking for recs if they are all new) so I will be taking awhile to watch all these. 
7. The Before Trilogy 
  - Second person to say that. Will definitely watch 
8. True Romance
9. In the mood for love 

## 2. Movies where the good guy turns into the bad guy

**Score:** 36
**Body:** Looking for movies that have the classic trope of "Good guy turns to bad guy". 

It doesn't necessarily have to be the main character in which this happens to by the way. 

Oh, and also. No big franchises like Star Wars, Marvel, DC, stuff like that. It can be a well known movie. Just none of the massive franchise stuff. However you can do franchises that aren't as popular/well known. Those are fine. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Chronicle is exactly this! Three guys get superpowers and one of them slowly becomes the villain. Its really well done and not part of any big franchise
2. Face Off - Literally 
3. Dune


But then again, Paul was never the good guy. 
4. Training Day
5. To Live and Die in LA.
6. Barry Lyndon 
7. *Falling Down* (1993). I haven't seen it in years, but it follows an ordinary man who snaps and spirals into violence across Los Angeles.
8. Pitch Black.

At the beginning Johns is portrayed kind of like a cop or guard, the de-facto leader who is trying to protect the group from the big bad killer Riddick.  The more you watch the more you realize he's actually a terrible person. 
9. Memento
10. [deleted]

## 3. Movies You Know Are Bad But Love Anyways

**Score:** 32
**Body:** title

I just finished watching Killer Klowns from Outer Space (1988) and absolutely loved it. The Return of the Living Dead (1985) and The Toxic Avenger (1984) are some other schlocky favorites. What are yours?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Stupid comedies 

Strange Brew (1983) Bob & Doug McKenzie’s misadventures 

Caveman (1981) with Ringo Starr. So silly 
  - Strange Brew is a top tier film. So quotable. 
2. I love Mystery Men. I didn't get all the hate when it came out. 
3. Masters of the Universe (1987)

Any of the 3 punisher movies.

Street Fighter (1994)

Judge Dredd (1995)
4. Dude, where’s my car. My wife and I know every line.
5. Joel Schumacher Batman movies. They may be a mess but they are a fun mess.
6. Beerfest 
7. Jaws: The Revenge is one of the worst movies ever made, but it’s still a favorite because it’s the first film I have fully conscious memories of seeing in a theater (I was around 5 1/2 the summer it was released).
8. Twilight. It’s not bad, but it’s ridiculous and I love it
9. I like The Love Guru.

I enjoy Mike Myers being silly. I also enjoyed The Pentaverate.

## 4. Cheesy absurd 1980s romance like Secret Admirer, or Mannequin

**Score:** 26
**Body:** Something that has a really ludicrous absurd fun plot, like a sorta romcom farce. Saw both of these recently and they are just super sweet and charming, but still pretty deranged n absurd. Love that sense of batshit cheesy 80s romance. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Once Bitten 1985

Can't Buy Me Love 1987

Girls Just Want to Have Fun 1985

Big Trouble in Little China 1986
2. Splash (1984)
3. Romancing the Stone
  - And Jewel of the Nile!
4. Better Off Dead
  - I still, to this day, say "I want my two dollars!" everytime I feel duped out of a small amount of $$! 

I'll also add One Crazy Summer to this list.
5. Weird Science
6. Electric Dreams
7. Earth Girls Are Easy
8. Weird Science

Teen Witch

My Science Project

Love Potion #9

## 5. Movie scenes that are great for film analysis?

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I need to analyze one scene from a movie for a school project I'm unsure what to pick though. I'm currently leaning towards the coin toss scene from no country for old men or a scene from parasite.

Any suggestions are welcome,

Thanks.

Edit: I will need to break down cinematography, lighting,  music, shot techniques and be able to talk about directors purpose and how these ideas impact the audience

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. steady cam shot from Goodfellas
  - Or the opening one shot from boogie nights. 
2. The botched assassination attempt in Miller's Crossing.


3. Rear Window (Hitchcock). He watches his neighbors through his back window and thinks he sees a murder.  The neighbors represent what conversation he is having or thoughts he is thinking 
4. Oh wait..one scene..oops


I was going to say Groundhog Day
5. I’d go for Dark City (1998) the opening scene including the goldfish. It gives a very neo-noir dystopian atmosphere designed to engage and engross the audience with curiosity.
6. Arrival

You'll have to watch the full movie to get the meaning though.
7. Good will hunting(1997) the room scene where he and his girlfriend are discussing

Possession (1981) tunnel scene (your teachers will love it) , arguably the best acting ever recorded

Scent of a woman (1992) Court scene
8.   Touch of Evil   1958 tracking  shot in the opening scene 
9. any scene from Return of the Jedi featuring Vader.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. This is the reunion we've been waiting for

**Score:** 4707

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cindy, the TV's leaking - Still lives rent free in my head
  - "This bitch is messing up my floors" 
    - I love in the first (third*) one when Cindy is talking to ghost face (brenda*) and he says “I know you did last summer” 

“It was Mardi Gras and I was out of beads!!”

Edit: oops. Had the movie wrong.
    - "Oh my god, Brenda! We're gonna die"
"Well, it just would've been you if you would've just shut the fuck up!"
2. And we don't question Brenda's immortality 
3. goddesses. sashimi. Toyota. Mitsubishi
  - Subaruuuuuu
4. Brenda is the funniest in these movies.
5. ![gif](giphy|12GzK1jYCaVCV2)
6. The screenshot made me realize I'm still not over my crush on Anna Faris

## 2. Do you think these 4 could play brothers in a movie?

**Score:** 1450

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Danny Devito and Arnold Schwarzenegger played brothers in a movie. 

  - Twins to be more precise 
  - Oh dang I thought that was a documentary. 
2. I prefer the original

https://preview.redd.it/c3a1dypecgng1.jpeg?width=189&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=1dedaeaf45228b683181aec0517c2777e525f114
  - The sequel will be called Four More Brothers 
3. Yep. Maybe Micheal Shannon be the dad? 
  - Jesus, perfect inbred family at that point 
4. I’m sorry, there are 4 different people here? The first row isn’t the same dude?!
5. Theon from GoT could be in this group of he’s not the bottom left guy.
6. They look like four AI responses to the same prompt.

## 3. What's a small error that doesn't affect the story but just bugs you too much.

**Score:** 253
**Body:** I love this movie for Cristina Ricci we are the same age and whse was my crush ever since Casper and Now & Then. This movie has it issues but the thing that annoys to no end is when Billy kidnaps her and takes her to her car and starts it with no problems and then complains about it being a "shifter" car and he can't drive it. If you've never to driven a manual car then you won't even pay attention here and nothing will stand out but if you have driving a manual car (hello Europeans) you'll know that you need to step on the clutch to turn it on and if you only drive automatics you know you don't have to even step on the brake let alone a non existent clutch so the fact that Billy turned on the car by stepping on the clutch and then acts like he's surprised by the manual transmission and can't drive it is just so stupid. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. In The Wedding Singer, the action takes place over the summer, Julia’s planned wedding to Glenn is in August. But the Robbie says to the limo driver (like a week or two before the wedding) that Julia took off her jacket in the limo because “She said it wasn’t jacket weather anymore.” Him remembering this quote is how the limo driver realizes Robbie likes Julia.

There is no transition from jacket weather to non-jacket weather in July-August in New Jersey.
  - Maybe Julia didn’t mean that “it wasn’t jacket weather” in the seasonal sense, but literally the individual day’s weather. Maybe it had rained that morning and it was a bit cooler when they picked her up, but by the time she took off the jacket in the afternoon it had warmed up. I mean it is possible to have cooler days even in July and August.
  - Good catch 
2. Butterfly Effect:  (spoilers as with every comment here.  Not gonna bother with the censored blocks)  
Background:  The main character discovers he can revisit key times in his life to do things drastically differently and thus drastically change his life and the lives of everyone he deals with—effectively being able to go to different timelines.  

The error:  At one point of the movie, the main character has landed himself in prison, which is problematic.  He needs the help of his cellmate and decides he needs to convince his cellmate of his ability.  So he does this by going back to his childhood and wounding his hands such that in the future he has scars that his cellmate interprets as holy stigmata, earning his cooperation….but this makes no sense, because the whole point is that the timeline is altered from that point on, so even if the action of badly scarring his hands didn’t change enough that his predicament would be different, in that new timeline he would’ve had those scars the entire time, but his cellmate specifically says that he saw the scars appear when the main character went to the past and came back.  Everything about this is janky as hell.
  - When he went back and stopped the mailbox bomb and became a limbless torso, nobody was surprised that his arms and legs suddenly disappeared. 
    - I figured because that event is actually extreme enough that it changed the events of his life after. You can see the relationship characters have is completely different compared to beforehand. 

The time in prison where he goes back to being a kid and scars his hands isn’t the sort of extreme that it changed his life. It just left a visible mark he didn’t have moments before. 

One’s changing the timeline and ones just an event within a timeline.
  - That specific flashback always bothered me too. You’re telling me that being a kid that willingly stabbed himself landed him in the exact same trajectory to be put in the same prison at the same time with the same cellmate? Where was the butterfly effect of that? I’m glad someone else brought it up
3. You only need to step on the clutch if it is left in gear (which is best practice as a back-up for the handbrake). If you leave it in neutral, no need for the clutch. 
4. My friend had a theory about this movie: Christina Ricci's character goes along reluctantly but easily with Gallo's character *because she overhears the remark about his giant penis just outside the bathroom*. 

I haven't rewatched it but this is the only detail I remember about this film. 
5. I love the movie and hate its awkwardness at the same time. He’s a very hateable character and a weirdo IRL but it’s an excellent film of its type.

Her little dancing scene is endearing.


## 4. War Machine (2026) Netflix's new original movie.

**Score:** 190
**Body:** Might be in the minority based on the RT and Letterboxd scores, but War Machine felt incredibly generic and a bit of a rip off of War of the Worlds (2005). Every scene was predictable with the usual cannon fodder supporting cast. But for me the most egregious thing was the surface level look at PTSD. There was an opportunity to create something meaningful and relatable, instead it went down the route of slop. Thoughts?

For those interested full review here [https://adamreviewsfilm.com/war-machine/](https://adamreviewsfilm.com/war-machine/)

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's basically the first Predator movie but with an alien robot.
  - Which, like, fuck yeah
  - Ya know that sounds pretty watchable 
2. Okay, am I crazy or did Netflix not produce a film titled War Machine in 2017 with Brad Pitt? About the military or something? 

Seems odd that no one thought to pick a different title. 
  - Almost like Marvel also has a character with the same name. 
    - Its that black fella in an Ironman suit aint it?
      - ![gif](giphy|tEQ8aAOh60us0)

Boom!  Ya lookin for this?
3. the fact that the alien robot scans the people only to then miss the shots is absolutely hilarious
4. "I can't carry the gun... but I can carry you!"
5. A simple, Friday night pop corn flick. Im sure some will bashed it, but it's alright.

## 5. What are the Best examples of characters who did nothing wrong but are still widely hated

**Score:** 173
**Body:** Chuck mcgill

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Nothing wrong?

![gif](giphy|ghuvaCOI6GOoTX0RmH)
2. Not sure he’s the best example. He did plenty wrong. 
3. ... Chuck was AT LEAST as monstrous as Kim, if not Jimmy himself.

He resented his brother from the day he was born. He blamed his brother for every misfortune in their life (Jimmy stole from their father but the entire town did as well). And when his brother was finally trying to get his life right, he did everything he could to destroy him. Whether that was actively preventing him from getting a law degree (hence University of American Samoa), preventing Howard from hiring Jimmy after he got said degree, or taking away the class action suit that Jimmy sought out and actively fought for.

Jimmy was an asshole. But basically the entire first arc (from meeting Nacho until "chicanery") is Jimmy actually trying to go legit. Even his scams are in the interest of The Good (mostly against the embezzlers). His Elder Law arc led to the class action suit to protect abused retirees. And Chuck saw that and did everything he could to trick Jimmy into giving that to HHM. Including manipulating Kim.

And you can see this in the end. When Chuck is served up to the legal insurance folk and becomes a liability to HHM, he manipulates and bullies Howard and threatens to destroy everything out of spite. He treats Howard, and HHM, as though they were just as evil as Jimmy. Because Chuck never truly hated Jimmy (even if he had good reason). He hated the idea of anyone getting attention or praise without him.


The great thing about Better Call Saul is that EVERYONE is monstrous. Except Howard. Howard was a good guy with prick-like tendencies.
  - Poor Howard
    - Howard should be the number one answer. 
4. No spoilers, but Better Call Saul has a major character who fits this desription a lot better. 
5. Scrappy-Doo (not counting the movie where he was behind it all)
6. What? Chuck was a total prick to Jimmy 
  - And basically tried to take down HHM because of his ego 
7. Col. Bellows on I Dream of Jeanie has always seemed like a great example of this. He rarely does anything bad or wrong, but he's the main antagonist and they're always trying to gaslight him into thinking he's literally insane. 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. I just finished Mulholland Drive and genuinely have no idea what I just watched

**Score:** 166
**Body:** I finally watched Mulholland Drive last night because it’s always on those “greatest movies ever made” lists. I went in expecting a complicated mystery… but I genuinely feel like my brain got scrambled.

For the first hour or so, I thought I understood what was happening. Then the story just kept getting stranger and stranger, and by the end I wasn’t even sure what parts were real, what parts were dreams, or if any of it actually happened.

Some things that completely lost me:

The whole shift in identities near the end

The blue box / blue key situation

Club Silencio (which felt important but I have no idea why)

Whether the first half of the movie is supposed to be a dream

I’m not even saying it’s bad, it was actually really fascinating and creepy but I feel like I’m missing something huge.

So for people who understand this movie…

What is actually going on?

Is there a generally accepted interpretation, or is it intentionally meant to be confusing?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Someone could probably explain this a lot better than me but I'll give it a quick go from what I remember:

Diane (Naomi Watts) and Camilla (Laura Harring) are aspiring actresses who had a sexual relationship with each other that meant a lot more to Diane than it did to Camilla. Eventually Camilla started to become successful and she essentially dropped Diane for the affections of Adam (Justin Theroux), a famous director. Diane became depressed and jealous and hired the hitman to kill Camilla, who told her that the job would be confirmed once she received a blue key. Upon seeing the key, she became guilty with what she had done and was driven to suicide.

Now, that's all covered in the second half of the movie, but the first half is the dream that Diane has in her dying moments, in which all of the details of her reality are moved around to create a more pleasant picture of her life in review. In this world, she's a much more charming and promising young actress named Betty who kills it in her audition and secures the undivided love of a clueless Camilla (aka Rita). Also, the arrogant Adam is a cuck, the hitman can't get his job done, and Diane Selwyn is just a random woman whose dead body they come across, eliminating all of her other real-life concerns. Some of the other details of the dream are more mysterious and unresolved, like the dream of the man in the diner and club silencio, but they can be seen as abstract hints to the lies and ugliness that Diane is  keeping from herself in this state.

In her dream, nasty truths like the meaning of the blue key are turned into fun little plot devices that you would see in a traditional mystery movie, while her record of being cheated on, traumatized, and eventually dying an unglorious death are projected onto other people. Essentially it helps her to have the classic yet unattainable "Hollywood Dream" that she had always wanted just before she dies, simply by being able to shift some stuff around.
  - Agree with all this except that the dream does not take place during her dying moments. At the start we see a POV of a head hitting a pillow, that’s Diane in a depressed state going to sleep. When the Cowboy says ‘time to wake up’ and we hear knocking sounds, that’s when Diane wakes up, because in reality her lesbian ex roommate is knocking on her front door.

She spends the day moping around her house, wracked with depression and guilt over murdering Camilla, and at the end she goes completely insane and blows her brains out.
    - Yeah, the first two hours are the dream (lol the pillow shot) and the last half hour explains the reality. It’s kind of like a reverse Wizard of Oz.
  - You nailed pretty much everything, just that the dream doesn't start in her dying moments but right before it. After the beginning swing dance scene it cuts to a blurry pov shot of someone in bed turning their head into the pillow and fading to black, which is Diane going to sleep and the fantasy dream starting. Then at the end when she wakes up is when she finds the key delivered to her on the table, starts fantasising about their relationship and sex, gets overcome with guilt which then manifests in that old couple chasing her before killing herself. 

And I think Diane Selwyn's corpse in the dream, since that's her name in reality, symbolises both her guilt and her impending doom because of it. As well as a kind of omen or foreshadowing that the dream is coming to an end due to her 'skeletons in the closet'. Which is also a similar thing I think the homeless man jump scare represents—the filthy, repressed guilt.

It's been a while but I think those are the sequence of events. Also, I had this thought just the other day about the Club Silencio scene—there's a lot of meta and surreal stuff going on with illusions and seeing through them. Perhaps it was a real event they both went to, and the 'NO HAY BANDA' performance helped Diane realise the illusion of her romanticised 'Hollywood dream' relationship with Camilla. And this is why she seizures and snaps out of the dream when reexperiencing it.
2. Another thing i would like to add is that for me Mulholland Drive feels like a sister movie to Lost Highway. Both have similar "dissociated state" like structure and similar themes, but from different perspectives. 
  - i'm happy to see somebody else saying this, it's something i been thinking about for like twenty years. two different treatments of the same idea of how do you live with yourself after doing something terrible to somebody, you either invent an entire fantasy world to live in until it falls apart around you, or you physically become a different person
3. I think approaching David Lynch films with "whodunnit" approach is kinda wrong approach to take. Sure, there are some quite plausible solving-the-puzzle explanations you can find online, but IMHO that's not the point of his films. Enjoying magical experience, subconscious mindfucks and having personal explanation is much more worthy and meaningful then having "what is actually going on" explained to you.
  - I watched this film for the first time when i was about 18 and never had an experience like it before. I was amazed at how i could enjoy a film so much and yet have no idea what was going on, i felt something subliminally.

But i immediately went to the internet to look up explainations and honestly that helped me appreciate the film even further. I dont necessarily see anything wrong with it, it didnt overly intellectualise the film or anything
  - I'd honestly recommend against reading any "complete" story explanation as it can limit your own ability to experience and interpret the film
4. I think I know the answer but the obvious question is if you've ever seen a David Lynch film before. He generally operates in the realm of surrealism, and his films are really things intended to be felt more than understood. Mulholland Drive is very open to interpretation, but the most important plot point to understand is that the first two thirds of the film are a dream, and when the cowboy says "Time to wake up, pretty girl", most if not all things after that are "real". The film fucking rules, I remember when I first saw it, I watched it like 20 times that month to try and figure out what its deal was.

Also some context for the film, it was originally intended to be a TV show. The initial concepts were for a Twin Peaks spin-off focused around the character Audrey, before becoming its own thing. The first two thirds of the film are effectively a pilot, but the TV studios rejected it. Lynch then got funding from Studio Canal to complete the work as a single feature film, so that also explains some of the disjointedness and plot threads that don't actually go anywhere

## 2. Life of Chuck - What is the Lifespan of a Movie Today?

**Score:** 30
**Body:** I hadn’t thought about this recently, and perhaps it’s because this is another Stephen King adaptation that made me think of it now, but what is the lifespan of a movie in today’s age.

The reason I ask this is because Shawshank Redemption was not a huge success until it hit the home video market and was constantly running on cable. There are countless other movie that everyone’s seen a million times because it was always on TV, and no matter when you started you would always watch to the end because it’s just always engaging.

I Feel like Life of Chuck is a perfect type of movie for that old school method, but movies aren’t consumed that way anymore. What are the chances of Life of Chuck becoming an always watchable classic? Or is it just another movie that will be lost in the void?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Life of Chuck was the first time I remained seated in the cinema for a good 5 or more minutes at the end of a film just trying to compose myself before leaving. A truly wonderful and unique film and I hope it will become the sleeper cult classic I suspect it might. It should hold up well on TV too since it didn't rely too much on visual grandiosity. Although I'm very glad I saw it in the cinema. The impact of the opening act was enhanced for sure. 
2. It does feel harder now for a movie to slowly grow the way The Shawshank Redemption did through cable and home video. Streaming tends to create shorter attention cycles, where something trends for a week and then disappears from the conversation. that said, movies like The Life of Chuck might still find their audience over time if people keep recommending them. the path just feels less organic now and more dependent on algorithms than late night channel surfing.
3. I really do think 98% of movies these days disappear into the void of streaming, and the 2% exceptions are usually pretty obvious because they were real successes in theaters (ie; Sinners will have a successful life on TV reruns. Of course it will, it made a lot of money and was widely loved when it first released). You can say it’s the kind of movie to be rewatched on cable, but that would require a cable channel buying rights to an independent drama film that didn’t make any money or win any awards (already pretty unlikely), and also people watching cable in the 2030s (more unlikely). 

Shawshank Redemption wasn’t a random unknown movie, it was nominated for many Oscar’s which was and probably still is the best way to market adult drama films to the mainstream public. It might have lost money in theaters but being a best picture nominee is an inherent hook, it makes cable channels want to buy the rights to play it and it makes people who rent DVDs (also not a thing anymore) more likely to check it out. If a sentimental drama catches on with cable/streaming rewatches over the next decade, something like The Holdovers or The Fabelmans would be a closer equivalent to Shawshank Redemption. The Life of Chuck will probably spend its entire existence in the back catalogue of various streaming services and mostly pop up when people are looking for what Tom Hiddleston movies are available to watch this month. 
4. 100% it will just continue on its trajectory of being a relatively obscure film, loved by the people who connected with it, occasionally “discovered” by people who stumbled onto it streaming or more dedicated movie watchers doing deeper dives into the filmographies of the people involved. 

5. I thought this movie was incredibly overrated and extremely low on character development, story, and anything resembling genuinely heartfelt stakes.

I was extremely surprised by how shallow it was, especially given what Mike Flanagan is capable of. I also felt that the metaphors were lazy, the sets looked cheap, and the costuming, obvious. Flanagan can do better. 
  - It lacks irony. Modern film watchers are so addicted to irony and sarcasm that they hate it when it’s not there. David foster Wallace called it irony poisoning. Critics will levy “saccharine” at it as if sincerity is a bad thing. Same thing happened to cloud atlas and other wachowsky sisters movies. They refuse to be ironic and people called it corny.
  - Same. Huge Flanagan fan and I really wanted to love this film but have finally given in to the fact that I just don't. Some strong moments, especially Act 3 but this is maybe the worst example I've yet found of Flanagan allowing the written word to get in the way of storytelling with a filmmaker's toolset. Over reliance on narration, unrealistic dialogue, monologues that prattle on forever. Purple prose in cinema form.


What I can say is that this got me to track down the short story for the first time, which was very moving and is honestly now one of my favorite King works. Really phenomenal little story.
  - Agree. Overly precious, saccharine. I liked the dance scene though lol
    - Who cares? Genuinely, what’s wrong with something being sweet and heartfelt?

Every critical review or comment I read about the film said it was “saccharine,” and I don’t get it. 
6. I hope it does. I found it to be one of the best films I saw last year. I think there's two types of people at home now, those who scroll through titles until one grabs them and those who have a specific title they're looking for, search and find that. I'm hoping with enough recommendations (I've told my whole family to see it) it will pick up steam, but I just don't know if people browsing will come across it buried in one of their many streaming services. Whoever is reading this, if you've not seen The Life of Chuck, do yourself a favor and watch it without knowing anything about it first

## 3. Survey: Interest in boutique Blu-ray releases of Indian films (1-minute survey)

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Hi everyone,

I’m doing some research on collector interest in boutique Blu-ray releases of Indian cinema and would really appreciate feedback from the physical media community.

Most boutique labels focus on Western or East Asian films, and I’m trying to gauge whether there is any demand for restored Indian films aimed at collectors.

I made a short 10-question survey (about 1 minute) covering things like:

* Blu-ray collecting habits
* interest in Indian cinema
* what collectors value in physical media
* potential demand for restored releases

If you collect Blu-rays or 4K discs, your input would be extremely helpful.

Survey link:  
[https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSddIjNL1meQbLpdIBBfHH5JXDHReXRmaKkq\_J7hpeVHrvoQBA/viewform?usp=sharing&ouid=105656807035454008875](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSddIjNL1meQbLpdIBBfHH5JXDHReXRmaKkq_J7hpeVHrvoQBA/viewform?usp=sharing&ouid=105656807035454008875)

Thanks in advance for your time — and if you have thoughts about Indian films that deserve a proper restoration or boutique release, I’d love to hear them in the comments too.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. Did anyone find Hamnet didn't look very good?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I wasn't the biggest fan of the movie as a whole, but I think from the outset the thing that really made me feel off about it was the cinematography and look of the film. 
The indoor scenes shot by candlelight are okay, but so many of the outdoor scenes just look really ugly, like someone just took a dslr on basic settings and didn't bother doing any colour grading.
There's a distinct digital look to the film which I find whenever it appears in a period film I just get turned off, many of the wide shots just look like a set. 

Now I'm not saying every historical film needs to look like Barry Lyndon, Zone of Interest is as bland looking as you can get but I find it works for that because the point of the film is to not be glamorous in the slightest. 

I really just felt like something was sorely missing visually from the film. One thing I loved about Marty Supreme was the visual look, it had such lovely flavour and texture, I was hoping for that in a film about Shakespeare. 


Also, not related to the visuals but something I found was a missed opportunity was to have done the film in more of an Original Pronunciation way, more similar to the accents of the time instead of doing the standard RP we see in all these Shakespeare related films. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think you are calling naturally lit cinematography style “bland” but I found the opposite. 

Then you also called The Zone of Interest cinematography “bland”?  Have to disagree on both. 
2. I understand why Zhao and Zal shot it how they did but I agree with you. The naturally lit parts of the film looked quite bad in my opinion. The flat lighting—which they prefer due to its realism—paired with the deep focus and a low contrast color scheme/grading just doesn’t give my eye anything to grab onto. Subjects often aren’t framed in a way that draws your eye to them, either. Again, this is supposed to enhance the naturalism but I just find it boring and ugly. 

Indoor scenes lit mostly with window light also had a ratcheting back and forth between flat darkness and a heavily backlit reserve shot that I found really grating.

The candlelit scenes are much better because they’re actually making choices about how to light the scene and they do a good job. 

It works for a lot of people, obviously, but I had the same hang ups. (Though calling Zone of Interest “bland” is insane. Zal’s style made perfect sense there and it suffered from basically none of these problems.) 

  - Yes I'm glad I'm not the only one. Bland isn't a complaint btw. Fargo Season 3 is bland looking because they removed all the blue but it works. 

Yeah, nothing draws your eye, I don't think Natural =/= Nothing should look intentional or eye catching. 
3. I think Hamnet was supposed to be low contrast, tonal colours etc to emphasise realism and intimacy vs theatrical lighting, I don't think that's the same thing as bland. I think you just like high contrast colours. 
4. i guess i get what you mean, but i thought the cinematography was very beautiful and i guess the colouring was fitting. the swimming scene kinda stuck out in my mind when i watched it for som3 reason.
5. I watched it in Dolby Vision and found the whole movie beautiful. The outdoor scenes were a lovely green, with the pops of that symbolism in red. It all was gorgeous.

How did you watch it? Maybe in a theatre the projecting was off with not enough light? That would make a picture look dull.

Overall I thought it looked very natural, but the forrest scenes definitely had bold colors to reflect that almost magical quality. 

## 5. Is there anyone out there who judges sound and music to be ancillary or unimportant to the medium of film?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Today I saw a claim on this sub that "sound and music are completely ancillary to \[the medium of film\]". This shocked me so I thought I'd ask the question here, then give my response.

I have three main claims about the prevailing patterns of the medium:

1. The actual existing medium of film has been audiovisual.
2. Sound and music have been normal and necessary to the production and artistic values of film works and to film comedy, drama, and documentary.
3. Critical film discourses have deemphasised sound and music to give attention to *visual* and *narrative* *or textual* structure and values.

Yes there are plenty of exceptions to the second claim. Some kinds of avant-garde film, such as those made by Stan Brakhage, have been intended for silent screenings.  Then there is « cinéma trouvé » like all the silent CCTV film out there.

Despite these I say my claims fairly describe how the medium has almost always worked, even before the end of the silent era.

I give some of my reasoning below. The embedded links are to references, or to clips or articles I think you should check out because they are fun or interesting.

I'd love to hear what you think about this topic. And also maybe someone's going to realise what an absurd view "sound and music don't really matter for the medium" is after reading this.

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**Sound and music in film production, media and presentation**

Even if you have a narrowly visual definition of the medium for some other purpose, many creative products in the medium, including almost all film comedies, dramas and documentaries consist of an audiovisual montage.

The etymology of the term the Lumières chose for the original *cinématographes*, roughly "movement-writers", might seem to imply a strictly visual orientation, but then we certainly [hear movement ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JHFXG3r_0B8)in most film productions.

The word *film* itself connotes vision for us too, but the very first patent for a [sound-on-film](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sound-on-film) recording technique predates the [Roundhay Garden Scene](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roundhay_Garden_Scene).

In the [silent film era ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silent_film)it was very rare for "silent" films to be presented without music. The early years of cinema came following decades of European mania for ["Wagnerism"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_films_using_the_music_of_Richard_Wagner). Silent films by DW Griffith, Buñuel and Hitchcock were all presented along with Wagner's music, suggesting a genealogy of opera and film as forms of art.

Now consider sound and music in film production. Both today and for most of its history the film industry has had specialist boom operators, [ADR engineers and editors](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_eBWSaw93RU), sound editors, [sound designers](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Hlc_0-vkCto), sound effects and [foley](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Rv0YPOFcwcI) [artists](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hRRGmhMGt9I), [composers](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BePfzCOMRZQ), musicians, music directors … as well as *screenwriters*, [*actors*](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vI8c3eSIkQY) and [*directors*](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VE03Lqm3nbI) … whose roles usually demand a contribution to the sound or music of the films they produce.

**Sound and music neglected in film theory, philosophy and science**

Film theory sometimes falls away when it comes to sound and music, leaving a negligent gap alongside visual theories and narrative or textual theories. Of course this isn't always true: there is also abundant theory connecting the medium and its sound and music. But sound and music receive less emphasis.

In visual theory, [the observable reciprocal organisation of film images with film sound and music](https://necsus-ejms.org/enhanced-echoes-digitisation-new-perspectives-film-sound/) is often neglected in favour of theories narrowed down to visual grammar or montage such as the [Kuleshov effect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kuleshov_effect), or the accepted role of DW Griffith in shot-making and editing innovations, or [Eisenstein's dialectical approach](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soviet_montage_theory), and so on.

At the other pole of narrative or textual analysis, attention to the sounds of a film are very often reduced to textual analysis of its dialogue, as in Zupančič’s famous theory joke about ["coffee without cream"](https://thephilosophicalsalon.com/you-are-not-having-milk/) derived from Greta Garbo's lines in Ernst Lubitsch's comedy [NINOTCHKA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ninotchka). Structural narrative analyses of film may take this drift further, losing interest in the medium-specificity of the entirety of a film's audiovisual montage and using film structure to illustrate theory, as in Žižek's "three floors of the Bates Motel" analysis of PSYCHO.

However, in film-centric philosophy we also have Deleuze's [CINEMA II: THE TIME-IMAGE](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cinema_2:_The_Time-Image). This work develops Deleuze's misleadingly named image-ontology with a theory of how film montage can create "autonomous" sonic-temporal relations between montage and audience.

In dragging this post together, I found there's a mature psychological and neuroscientific literature studying the role of sound and music in the affects of film as a medium. ["Music as a source of emotion in film"](https://psycnet.apa.org/record/2001-05534-005) by Annabel J Cohen gives an example.

**Film soundtracks and themes and their uses**

There is an empirically familiar and resounding importance of the [film soundtracks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films) of the large majority of the highest-grossing films of all time.

Film and television scores also very often incorporate ["character themes"](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vDFj5FD5SP0), "location themes" or a grammar of musical affect [structured](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Film_score#Structure) together with the visual montage to call back to or cue specific character entrances or involvement, define the intentions or role of a character, declare the start or climax of an action sequence, ratchet up the suspense or shocks of a  jump or gore horror sequence, delineate a romance or sex scene, declare a narrative epilogue, etc.

In many cases film or television stories lose their navigability or have their meanings of affects transformed if these musical cues are transformed or changed.

Not coincidentally the practice of integrating musical themes of this kind with narrative and montage is often traced back to Wagner's concept of [Leitmotiv](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leitmotif).

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Can you make a movie without sound? Yes. Can you make a movie without image? No. That is the definition of ancillary—sound can support the image, but it’s not integral.

Cinema was screened without audio of any kind from the 1890s through the 1910s, and even through the 1920s often without musical accompaniment.

If an art museum features music playing in a gallery exhibit of paintings, does that make a painting an audio/visual work?

Common practice does not define a medium. Filmmaking and cinema are fundamentally visual media. It’s not a controversial opinion.
